title: Hematopoietic Stem Cells
authors: David T. Scadden, Dan L. Longo

items.subtopic: Hematopoietic Stem Cells | CARDINAL FUNCTIONS OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS
items.title: Why Hematopoietic Stem Cell Function is Critical for Survival
items.content: Hematopoietic stem cells are essential for producing all blood cell types and some tissue cells. If these stem cells are damaged, a person would survive only 2–4 weeks without extraordinary support measures.
keyPoints: All blood and some tissue cells derive from hematopoietic stem cells.
Without functional hematopoietic stem cells, survival is limited to 2–4 weeks absent extraordinary support.
Clinical use of these stem cells saves tens of thousands of lives annually.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · Hematopoietic Stem Cells | CARDINAL FUNCTIONS OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS

items.subtopic: Hematopoietic Stem Cells | CARDINAL FUNCTIONS OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS
items.title: How Hematopoietic Stem Cells Maintain Blood Cell Production
items.content: Despite the high demand for blood cell production, hematopoietic stem cells maintain a small pool of 20,000–200,000 cells to produce hundreds of billions of blood cells daily through self-renewal and differentiation.
keyPoints: Stem cells produce hundreds of billions of blood cells daily from a pool estimated at 20,000–200,000.
Self-renewal ensures the stem cell population is sustained over time.
Differentiation produces mature cells necessary for tissue function.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · Hematopoietic Stem Cells | CARDINAL FUNCTIONS OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS

items.subtopic: DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS | MOBILITY OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS
items.title: Sites and Timing of Hematopoietic Stem Cell Development
items.content: Hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) develop in a sequential order starting from the yolk sac to the fetal liver, then to bone marrow and spleen. This progression is crucial for the changing types of blood cells produced at each site.
keyPoints: Yolk sac produces red cells with embryonic hemoglobin and tissue-resident macrophages.
Intraembryonic sites generate stem cells, red cells, platelets, and innate immunity cells.
Adaptive immunity cells are robustly produced as the thymus forms and bone marrow is colonized in the second trimester.
Stem cell proliferation remains high until shortly after birth.
HSCs circulate throughout life, with brief circulation times (minutes in mice).
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS | MOBILITY OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS

items.subtopic: DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS | MOBILITY OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS
items.title: Mechanisms of Hematopoietic Stem Cell Mobilization and Engraftment
items.content: Hematopoietic stem cells enter and exit the bone marrow through molecular interactions, involving selectins, integrins, chemokines, and calcium-sensing receptors. These mechanisms are crucial for both developmental migration and therapeutic mobilization.
keyPoints: Circulating HSCs engage P- and E-selectin via CD162 and CD44 to slow movement (rolling phenotype).
VCAM-1 on stem cells engages endothelial VLA-4 for firm adhesion.
CXCL12 interacts with CXCR4 receptors, aiding in engraftment from circulation to bone marrow.
In adults, CXCR4 is crucial for retention and mobilization of HSCs.
Therapeutic mobilization uses G-CSF and plerixafor (blocks CXCR4) for leukapheresis.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS | MOBILITY OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS

items.subtopic: HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL MICROENVIRONMENT
items.title: Role of the Hematopoietic Stem Cell Niche in Transplantation Success
items.content: The hematopoietic stem cell niche is crucial for successful bone marrow transplantation. It provides regulatory signals that allow transplanted stem cells to thrive, expand, and produce daughter cells while preventing unregulated growth due to their undifferentiated state.
keyPoints: Niche serves as both a nurturing site and imposes limits on stem cell proliferation.
Perivascular space in trabecular bone is a key niche location within the bone marrow.
Kit ligand and CXCL12 produced by mesenchymal and endothelial cells are vital for hematopoietic stem cells.
Endosteal region is particularly important for transplanted cells due to disruption of central marrow during conditioning regimens.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL MICROENVIRONMENT

items.subtopic: HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL MICROENVIRONMENT
items.title: Impact of Niche Alterations on Hematopoiesis and Disease
items.content: Alterations in the hematopoietic stem cell niche can significantly impact hematopoiesis and may contribute to disease. Experimental models suggest that mutations in niche cells can lead to myeloid malignancies, highlighting the importance of the niche's integrity for normal blood production.
keyPoints: Niche regulates stem cell numbers by balancing nurture and constraints.
Various cell types (e.g., macrophages, osteoclasts) and extracellular matrix proteins influence stem cell function.
Endosteal region disruption during transplantation conditioning affects niche functionality.
Mutations in niche cells can lead to myeloid malignancies, indicating the niche's role in disease.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL MICROENVIRONMENT

items.subtopic: EXCESS CAPACITY OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS
items.title: Why Hematopoietic Stem Cells Never Deplete in Healthy Individuals
items.content: Hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) have an excess capacity that ensures they do not deplete under normal conditions. This is evidenced by serial transplantation studies in mice and long-term outcomes in allogeneic transplant recipients, who maintain adequate blood cell production over decades.
keyPoints: Serial transplantation studies suggest HSCs can reconstitute several animals successively with normal blood cell production.
Allogeneic stem cell transplant recipients maintain blood cells for decades, indicating sufficient initial stem cell numbers.
Regulatory mechanisms differ between stem cells and more mature progenitor cells.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · EXCESS CAPACITY OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS | HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL DIFFERENTIATION

items.subtopic: EXCESS CAPACITY OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS
items.title: How Growth Factors Differentially Affect Stem Cells vs. Mature Blood Cells
items.content: Growth factors like erythropoietin and granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) have limited effects on stem cells compared to mature blood cell precursors, highlighting distinct regulatory mechanisms.
keyPoints: Erythropoietin stimulates red blood cell production but does not affect HSCs.
Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor promotes granulocyte precursor proliferation without altering HSC cycling.
Molecules like CXCL12 alter stem cell location indirectly, affecting niche interactions.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · EXCESS CAPACITY OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS | HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL DIFFERENTIATION

items.subtopic: HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL DIFFERENTIATION
items.title: Characteristics of Hematopoietic Stem Cell Differentiation and Proliferation
items.content: Hematopoietic stem cells differentiate into various mature blood cell types through intrinsic gene expression changes and external signals. This process involves a loss of plasticity and proliferative capacity as cells mature, with exceptions in lymphoid cells.
keyPoints: Differentiation occurs via intrinsic gene changes and niche/cytokine-directed signals.
Mature granulocytes cannot proliferate; proliferation is driven by progenitors.
Lymphoid cells retain proliferative ability linked to antigen recognition.
Hematopoietic progenitor differentiation takes ~10–14 days in humans.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · EXCESS CAPACITY OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS | HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL DIFFERENTIATION

items.subtopic: SELF-RENEWAL AND CLONAL DYNAMICS
items.title: Why Self-Renewal Increases Cancer Risk in Hematopoietic Stem Cells
items.content: Self-renewal allows hematopoietic stem cells to divide while maintaining an undifferentiated state, which is crucial for producing a vast number of mature blood cells. However, this also means that genetic mutations occurring in these stem cells can persist and accumulate over time due to their self-renewing nature, increasing the risk of cancer.
keyPoints: Stem cells divide asymmetrically, with one daughter cell continuing as a stem cell and the other differentiating.
Mutations in stem cells are more likely to persist compared to progenitor cells due to self-renewal.
The potential for genetic mutations in stem cells can lead to cancer development.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · SELF-RENEWAL AND CLONAL DYNAMICS

items.subtopic: SELF-RENEWAL AND CLONAL DYNAMICS
items.title: How Aging Affects Hematopoietic Stem Cell Clonal Dynamics
items.content: Aging leads to an increase in somatic mutations within hematopoietic stem cells, with an estimated 17 new mutations per year. This results in a higher prevalence of mutated clones that contribute significantly to blood cell production by age 70, potentially reducing the diversity and function of these cells.
keyPoints: Hematopoietic stem cells acquire approximately 17 somatic mutations annually.
By age 70, mutated or variant alleles are present in over 1% of blood cells.
10-20 clones contribute to 30-60% of blood cells by age 70, reducing clonal diversity.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · SELF-RENEWAL AND CLONAL DYNAMICS

items.subtopic: SELF-RENEWAL AND CLONAL DYNAMICS
items.title: Why Epigenetic Mutations are Common in Clonal Hematopoiesis
items.content: Epigenetic regulatory genes such as DNMT3a, TET2, and ASXL1 frequently harbor mutations in clonal hematopoiesis. These mutations affect DNA methylation and chromatin structure, which are crucial for maintaining the balance and function of hematopoietic stem cells.
keyPoints: Common mutations in clonal hematopoiesis involve epigenetic regulatory genes like DNMT3a, TET2, and ASXL1.
These mutations impact DNA methylation and chromatin structure, affecting transcription regulation.
Epigenetic control is vital for the homeostasis of the hematopoietic stem cell pool.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · SELF-RENEWAL AND CLONAL DYNAMICS

items.subtopic: THE RELATIONSHIP OF STEM CELLS TO CANCER | STEM CELLS AS TARGETS OF GENE THERAPY OR GENE EDITING
items.title: Stem-like Cells in Cancer and Relapse
items.content: Some cancers, like myeloid leukemias, have a hierarchy with stem-like cells that can self-renew and differentiate. These cells may be responsible for disease relapse post-therapy due to their unique molecular features making them less vulnerable.
keyPoints: Stem-like cells in cancer can initiate new tumors when transplanted into immunodeficient animals.
These cells are hypothesized to cause disease relapse after therapy.
Therapies targeting stem-like cells rather than the bulk of cancer cells may improve cure rates.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · THE RELATIONSHIP OF STEM CELLS TO CANCER | STEM CELLS AS TARGETS OF GENE THERAPY OR GENE EDITING

items.subtopic: THE RELATIONSHIP OF STEM CELLS TO CANCER | STEM CELLS AS TARGETS OF GENE THERAPY OR GENE EDITING
items.title: Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Leukemia
items.content: Hematopoietic stem cells, due to their self-renewal capability, are likely sources of leukemias. They can accumulate genetic mutations that lead to malignancy, although some mature populations with less defined self-renewal can also become malignant.
keyPoints: Stem cells are more prone to malignancy following oncogenic mutations.
Cancer stem-like cells may not originate from normal stem cells; they might acquire self-renewal through mutation.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · THE RELATIONSHIP OF STEM CELLS TO CANCER | STEM CELLS AS TARGETS OF GENE THERAPY OR GENE EDITING

items.subtopic: THE RELATIONSHIP OF STEM CELLS TO CANCER | STEM CELLS AS TARGETS OF GENE THERAPY OR GENE EDITING
items.title: Gene Therapy and Gene Editing in Hematopoietic Stem Cells
items.content: Hematopoietic stem cells are ideal targets for gene therapy due to their lifelong persistence. Genetic modifications can treat disorders like hemoglobinopathies or immunodeficiencies. Challenges include the quiescence of stem cells, but therapies exist for conditions such as cerebral adrenoleukodystrophy, $\beta$ thalassemia, and sickle cell anemia.
keyPoints: Gene therapy involves integrating a full coding sequence into hematopoietic stem cells.
CRISPR-Cas9 technology allows gene editing, such as reducing BLC11A expression to increase fetal hemoglobin in $\beta$ thalassemia and sickle cell anemia.
Current therapies require myeloablative conditioning but efforts are underway for in vivo gene editing.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · THE RELATIONSHIP OF STEM CELLS TO CANCER | STEM CELLS AS TARGETS OF GENE THERAPY OR GENE EDITING

items.subtopic: Hematopoietic Stem Cells | CARDINAL FUNCTIONS OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS
items.question: What would likely happen to a person if their hematopoietic stem cells are damaged and can no longer function?
options: The person would survive indefinitely with extraordinary support measures.
The person would survive 2–4 weeks in the absence of extraordinary support measures.
The person's immune system would immediately become fully functional again.
The person would not experience any significant health issues.
items.explanation: Without functioning hematopoietic stem cells, a person would survive only 2–4 weeks without extraordinary support measures.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · Hematopoietic Stem Cells | CARDINAL FUNCTIONS OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS

items.subtopic: Hematopoietic Stem Cells | CARDINAL FUNCTIONS OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS
items.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the cardinal functions of hematopoietic stem cells?
items.explanation: The cardinal functions are self-renewal and differentiation, not immune response activation.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · Hematopoietic Stem Cells | CARDINAL FUNCTIONS OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS

items.subtopic: Hematopoietic Stem Cells | CARDINAL FUNCTIONS OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS
items.question: What is the result of asymmetric cell division in hematopoietic stem cells?
options: Two cells destined for differentiation are produced.
One stem cell and one differentiating cell are produced.
items.explanation: Asymmetric cell division results in one stem cell and one differentiating cell.

options: Facilitating cell movement to the thymus
Retaining stem cells within the bone marrow
Enhancing proliferation in the fetal liver
Promoting differentiation into red blood cells
items.explanation: In adults, CXCR4 is crucial for retaining hematopoietic stem cells in the bone marrow.

items.explanation: Firm adhesion is accomplished by VCAM-1 on stem cells engaging endothelial VLA-4.

items.question: What is the primary role of granulocyte colony-stimulating factor in hematopoiesis?
options: It stimulates red blood cell production.
It drives rapid proliferation of granulocyte precursors.
It alters gene expression in stem cells.
It increases survival of mature lymphoid cells.
items.explanation: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor drives the rapid proliferation of granulocyte precursors but has little or no effect on stem cell cycling.

items.explanation: Stem cell quiescence, influenced by high levels of cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitors and niche signals, limits expansion.

items.question: Which type of mutations are most commonly associated with clonal hematopoiesis?

They can persist for the lifetime of an individual.

items.reason: Mature granulocytes increase in number through their own proliferation.
items.explanation: Blood cell proliferation is largely done by more immature progenitor populations, not mature cells like granulocytes.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · EXCESS CAPACITY OF HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELLS | HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL DIFFERENTIATION

items.subtopic: SELF-RENEWAL AND CLONAL DYNAMICS
items.assertion: Hematopoietic stem cells acquire somatic mutations over time.
items.reason: These mutations can provide a fitness advantage to the stem cell, leading to clonal expansion.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains why some clones expand due to advantageous mutations.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · SELF-RENEWAL AND CLONAL DYNAMICS

items.subtopic: SELF-RENEWAL AND CLONAL DYNAMICS
items.assertion: Hematopoietic stem cells are resistant to cytokines.
items.reason: This resistance is due to high levels of cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitors like p57.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain cytokine resistance.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · SELF-RENEWAL AND CLONAL DYNAMICS

items.subtopic: THE RELATIONSHIP OF STEM CELLS TO CANCER | STEM CELLS AS TARGETS OF GENE THERAPY OR GENE EDITING
items.assertion: Hematopoietic stem cells are less susceptible to gene integration events.
items.reason: Their relative quiescence makes them less amenable to molecular events necessary for integration or editing.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why hematopoietic stem cells are less susceptible.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · THE RELATIONSHIP OF STEM CELLS TO CANCER | STEM CELLS AS TARGETS OF GENE THERAPY OR GENE EDITING

items.subtopic: THE RELATIONSHIP OF STEM CELLS TO CANCER | STEM CELLS AS TARGETS OF GENE THERAPY OR GENE EDITING
items.assertion: Gene editing for hemoglobinopathies involves adding a new gene to the genome.
items.reason: CRISPR-Cas technology is used to delete regions of genes like BLC11A.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because gene editing involves deletion, not addition; however, the reason correctly describes the process.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Stem Cells · THE RELATIONSHIP OF STEM CELLS TO CANCER | STEM CELLS AS TARGETS OF GENE THERAPY OR GENE EDITING
